_meta.description: YouTube Quiz Database fuer ALLE public Videos - remAIke.TV
_meta.note: Quizzes müssen MANUELL im YouTube Studio eingetragen werden (keine API verfügbar). Quizzes können nach dem Speichern NICHT editiert werden (nur löschen + neu erstellen).
_meta.how_to: YouTube Studio → Video → Details → Quiz-Bereich rechts unten → 'Quiz hinzufügen'
_meta.limits: Max 10 Quizzes/Video, 2-4 Antworten, genau 1 richtig, Erklärung max 500 Zeichen
_meta.strategy: Series-Template: gleiche Quizzes fuer alle Videos einer Serie. Individuelle Quizzes fuer Top-Performer.

quizzes.question: What makes White Zombie (1932) historically significant?

answers.text: First feature-length zombie film ever made
First film to win a horror award

quizzes.explanation: White Zombie (1932), directed by Victor Halperin and starring Bela Lugosi, is recognized as the first feature-length zombie film in cinema history. It was filmed on a budget of approximately $50,000.

quizzes.question: How long did it take to film White Zombie?
quizzes.explanation: White Zombie was filmed in just 11 days in March 1932 at the Universal Studios lot. The production borrowed sets from Dracula (1931), Frankenstein (1931), and The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1923) to save money.

quizzes.question: Which famous rock band took their name from this film?
quizzes.explanation: Rob Zombie named his band 'White Zombie' directly after this 1932 film. The band was active from 1985 to 1998 and became one of the most successful heavy metal acts of the 1990s.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (15/175): Felix at the Circus (1921)

quizzes.question: When was Felix the Cat first created?
quizzes.explanation: Felix the Cat first appeared in 'Feline Follies' on November 9, 1919, created by Pat Sullivan's studio. Animator Otto Messmer is widely credited as Felix's true creative father.

quizzes.question: What historic role did Felix the Cat play in television history?

answers.text: First image ever broadcast on TV (1928)
Star of the first TV commercial

quizzes.explanation: In 1928, a Felix the Cat figurine was used as the test image for the first experimental television broadcast by RCA, rotating on a turntable for hours. Felix literally was the first 'TV star'!

quizzes.question: Who is the real creator of Felix the Cat's design?
quizzes.explanation: Though Pat Sullivan took credit as studio owner, animator Otto Messmer is now recognized as Felix's true creator. Sullivan ran the business side while Messmer designed and animated Felix.

quizzes.question: What ability did the Fleischer Superman cartoons give Superman that he didn't have in the comics?
quizzes.explanation: In the original comics, Superman could only 'leap tall buildings in a single bound.' The Fleischer brothers found animating repeated leaping too difficult, so they gave him the power of flight — which the comics then adopted!

quizzes.question: How much did each Fleischer Superman cartoon cost to produce?
quizzes.explanation: The Fleischer Superman cartoons cost approximately $50,000 each — about four times the typical cartoon budget of the era. This allowed for the stunning rotoscoped animation and elaborate action sequences.

quizzes.question: How many Superman cartoons did Fleischer/Famous Studios produce in total?
quizzes.explanation: Fleischer Studios produced 9 Superman shorts, then Famous Studios (which took over) produced 8 more, for a total of 17 shorts between 1941 and 1943. They were nominated for an Academy Award.

quizzes.question: What film movement did 'The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari' (1920) launch?
quizzes.explanation: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari (1920) is considered the first German Expressionist film and arguably the 'first true horror film' according to Roger Ebert. It heavily influenced the film noir genre of the 1940s-50s.

quizzes.question: What is unique about the sets in Dr. Caligari?

answers.text: Shadows and light are painted directly on canvas
They were filmed on real streets

quizzes.explanation: The film's distorted, angular sets were painted on flat canvas — including the shadows! This was partly an artistic choice and partly a cost-saving measure, as painted canvases were cheaper than building 3D sets. Budget was only ~$12,000.

quizzes.question: How long did Dr. Caligari play continuously in one Paris cinema?
quizzes.explanation: The Cabinet of Dr. Caligari played in one Paris theatre for 7 consecutive years — a record that stood until 'Emmanuelle' in 1974. The film has a 96% score on Rotten Tomatoes based on 80 reviews.

quizzes.question: What Guinness World Record did 'Dinner for One' hold?

answers.text: Most frequently repeated TV programme ever

quizzes.explanation: Dinner for One was recognized as the most frequently repeated TV programme in the world. First recorded by NDR in Hamburg in 1963, it became a New Year's Eve tradition in Germany from 1972 onwards.

quizzes.question: How many drinks does butler James consume during the sketch?
quizzes.explanation: James must impersonate 4 absent (deceased) dinner guests, drinking a toast for each across 4 courses: sherry, white wine, champagne, and port — totaling 16 drinks! No wonder he keeps tripping over the tiger-skin rug.

quizzes.question: In which country is 'Dinner for One' virtually unknown, despite being performed in English?
quizzes.explanation: Despite being performed entirely in English by British actors Freddie Frinton and May Warden, the sketch was almost completely unknown in the UK until Sky Arts first aired it nationally in 2018. The BBC had declined it!

quizzes.question: What is 'Gymkhana' in the context of Ken Block's videos?

answers.text: Precision stunt driving with drifts, donuts and jumps
A type of rally race stage

quizzes.explanation: Ken Block's Gymkhana videos feature precision stunt driving through obstacle courses, combining drifting, donuts, and jumps. The Gymkhana series became a viral phenomenon, amassing billions of views on YouTube.

quizzes.question: What was Ken Block's professional background before becoming famous for Gymkhana?
quizzes.explanation: Ken Block co-founded DC Shoes in 1994 with Damon Way. He used his success in the shoe business to fund his rally racing career, eventually becoming one of the most recognizable figures in motorsport through his Gymkhana videos.

quizzes.question: Why is Charade (1963) often called 'the best Hitchcock film that Hitchcock never made'?

answers.text: It masterfully combines mystery, romance & comedy in Hitchcock's style
Hitchcock was originally scheduled to direct it
It uses Hitchcock's actual film crew
It's a remake of a Hitchcock film

quizzes.explanation: Directed by Stanley Donen and starring Cary Grant and Audrey Hepburn, Charade (1963) is often called 'the best Hitchcock film Hitchcock never made' due to its perfect blend of suspense, humor, and romance in the Hitchcock tradition.

quizzes.question: Why is Charade in the public domain despite being a major Hollywood film?

answers.text: Universal Pictures failed to include a copyright notice
It was intentionally released for free

quizzes.explanation: Charade fell into the public domain because Universal Pictures failed to include the required copyright notice on the film prints — a simple oversight under the copyright laws of the time that made this Cary Grant/Audrey Hepburn classic free for everyone.

quizzes.question: Who is the star of 'The Grim Game' (1919)?

answers.text: Harry Houdini, the famous escape artist

quizzes.explanation: The Grim Game stars Harry Houdini, the legendary escape artist and illusionist. It features a real mid-air plane collision that occurred accidentally during filming — one of the most dangerous stunts in silent film history.

quizzes.question: What extraordinary real event happened during filming of The Grim Game?

answers.text: Two airplanes actually collided mid-air during a stunt
Houdini nearly drowned in a water tank

quizzes.explanation: During filming, two biplanes accidentally collided mid-air while shooting an aerial sequence. This real crash was captured on film and incorporated into the movie, making it one of the most dangerous accidents in early cinema history.

quizzes.question: Which famous Batman actors appear in this Christmas special?

answers.text: Adam West and Burt Ward (the 1966 TV series)

quizzes.explanation: This Christmas special features Adam West as Batman and Burt Ward as Robin from the iconic 1966 TV series, known for its campy, colorful style with 'POW!' and 'BAM!' action effects.

quizzes.question: Who performs the famous song 'Minnie the Moocher' featured in this Betty Boop cartoon?
quizzes.explanation: Jazz legend Cab Calloway performs 'Minnie the Moocher' in this 1932 classic. The Fleischer Studios used rotoscoping — tracing over Calloway's actual dance movements — to animate the walrus ghost character in the cave sequence.

quizzes.question: What was Betty Boop originally designed as before becoming human?
quizzes.explanation: Betty Boop first appeared on August 9, 1930 in 'Dizzy Dishes' as a French poodle character with floppy dog ears. She was gradually redesigned into her iconic human form, with her dog ears becoming hoop earrings.

quizzes.question: How many Betty Boop cartoons were produced in total?
quizzes.explanation: Fleischer Studios produced approximately 90 Betty Boop cartoons between 1930 and 1939. The character's personality changed dramatically in 1934 due to the Motion Picture Production Code (Hays Code), making her more 'wholesome.'

quizzes.question: Who provided the voice for Superman in the Fleischer cartoons?
quizzes.explanation: Bud Collyer voiced Superman and Clark Kent. He cleverly used a deep, strong voice for Superman and a milder, higher-pitched voice for Clark Kent. He also famously performed the 'This looks like a job for Superman!' transition.

quizzes.question: Which military unit uses Felix the Cat as their mascot?
quizzes.explanation: The US Navy fighter squadron VFA-31 'Tomcatters' has used Felix the Cat as their mascot since the 1940s. Felix appears on their aircraft, carrying a bomb — one of the longest-running military mascot traditions in the US.

quizzes.question: What makes 'Getaway in Stockholm' unique among car chase films?

answers.text: It shows real illegal street racing through Stockholm at night
It was filmed entirely with drones
It was sponsored by the Swedish police

quizzes.explanation: Getaway in Stockholm became a viral underground sensation showing what appears to be real high-speed driving through the streets of Stockholm, Sweden at night. The identity of the driver was kept anonymous.

quizzes.question: Why was the original Nosferatu (1922) almost completely destroyed?

answers.text: Bram Stoker's estate sued — court ordered all copies burned
A studio fire destroyed the negatives

quizzes.explanation: Nosferatu was an unauthorized adaptation of Bram Stoker's Dracula. Stoker's widow sued, and a court ordered all copies destroyed. Fortunately, some prints had already been distributed internationally and survived.

quizzes.question: What innovative shadow technique did Nosferatu pioneer in horror cinema?

answers.text: Using the monster's shadow on walls to create fear without showing the creature

quizzes.explanation: Director F.W. Murnau pioneered the technique of showing Count Orlok's elongated shadow creeping up staircases and walls — creating terror through suggestion rather than direct sight. This technique influenced horror filmmaking for over a century.

quizzes.question: Who played the terrifying Count Orlok?
quizzes.explanation: Max Schreck played Count Orlok with such eerie realism that rumors persisted he was an actual vampire. His name 'Schreck' means 'terror' or 'fright' in German. The 2000 film 'Shadow of the Vampire' played on these myths.

quizzes.question: What type of cartoon is 'Tokyo Jokio' (1943)?
quizzes.explanation: Tokyo Jokio is a WWII-era Merrie Melodies propaganda cartoon produced by Warner Bros. in 1943. Like many cartoons of the era, it was created to boost American morale during the war. Today it serves as a historical document of wartime media.

quizzes.question: What was Buster Keaton's famous nickname?

answers.text: 'The Great Stone Face' (never smiled on camera)

quizzes.explanation: Buster Keaton was nicknamed 'The Great Stone Face' because he maintained a deadpan, expressionless face throughout his films — even during the most dangerous stunts. He performed all his own stunts without a stuntman.

quizzes.question: Who reportedly gave young Buster Keaton his nickname 'Buster'?
quizzes.explanation: According to legend, Harry Houdini saw young Joseph Keaton (age ~6 months) tumble down a flight of stairs without crying and exclaimed 'That was a real buster!' — and the nickname stuck for life.

quizzes.question: Who composed the original 'Peter and the Wolf' musical piece?
quizzes.explanation: Sergei Prokofiev composed 'Peter and the Wolf' in 1936 as a children's musical piece for the Moscow Central Children's Theatre. Each character is represented by a different instrument: Peter by strings, the bird by flute, the duck by oboe, the cat by clarinet, and the wolf by French horns.

quizzes.question: What makes 'Airship Destroyed' (1909) significant in film history?

answers.text: It's one of the earliest science fiction films ever made
It was the first color film
It was the first film shown in a cinema
It featured the first movie star

quizzes.explanation: Made in 1909, 'Airship Destroyed' (also known as 'The Aerial Torpedo') is one of the earliest science fiction films, showing the destruction of an airship using pioneering special effects techniques — decades before the rise of the sci-fi genre.

quizzes.question: What makes the 1916 version of '20,000 Leagues Under the Sea' groundbreaking?

answers.text: It features the first underwater footage ever filmed for a movie
It was the first film in color
It was the first Jules Verne adaptation
It used the first animatronic creature

quizzes.explanation: The 1916 film '20,000 Leagues Under the Sea' is historically significant for including some of the first underwater cinematography ever filmed for a feature. The Williamson brothers developed a special underwater tube and chamber for the filming.

videos.title: Voyage to the Planet of Prehistoric Women (1968)

quizzes.question: What unusual production method was used to create this 1968 film?

answers.text: It reuses footage from a 1962 Soviet sci-fi film with new scenes added
It was filmed in a single day
It was created by film students

quizzes.explanation: This film was created by taking footage from the 1962 Soviet science fiction film 'Planeta Bur' (Planet of Storms) and adding new scenes with American actors, including Mamie Van Doren. This 're-editing Soviet films' technique was used for several Roger Corman productions.

quizzes.question: Why is Winsor McCay's 'Little Nemo' (1911) considered a milestone?

answers.text: It's one of the first animated films ever made
It was the first animated film with sound
It was the first animated feature-length film
It was the first stop-motion film

quizzes.explanation: Winsor McCay's 'Little Nemo' (1911) is one of the earliest known animated films. McCay drew roughly 4,000 individual drawings on rice paper for the animation — a staggering effort when the concept of animation barely existed.

quizzes.question: What was Winsor McCay's 'day job' besides being an animation pioneer?
quizzes.explanation: Winsor McCay was a celebrated newspaper comic strip artist, best known for 'Little Nemo in Slumberland' (1905-1914) in the New York Herald. His animation work was a side project — yet he pioneered the entire art form.

quizzes.question: How many Commodore 64 computers were sold worldwide?
quizzes.explanation: The Commodore 64, released in 1982, holds the Guinness World Record for the highest-selling single computer model of all time, with estimates ranging from 12.5 to 17 million units sold worldwide.

quizzes.question: What does the '64' in Commodore 64 stand for?

answers.text: Model number 64 in the product line

quizzes.explanation: The '64' refers to the computer's 64 kilobytes of RAM — a generous amount for a home computer in 1982. The Commodore 64 was priced at $595 at launch and its famous SID sound chip became legendary among musicians and chiptune artists.

quizzes.question: Who directed 'Scarlet Street' (1945)?
quizzes.explanation: Fritz Lang, the legendary German-American director known for 'Metropolis' (1927) and 'M' (1931), directed Scarlet Street. It stars Edward G. Robinson as a mild-mannered cashier drawn into a web of deception and murder.

quizzes.question: Why was 'Scarlet Street' controversial upon its release?

answers.text: It was banned in several US cities for its dark themes
It featured the first on-screen kiss
It was filmed without a script

quizzes.explanation: Scarlet Street was banned in New York, Milwaukee, and Atlanta for its dark themes of murder, deception, and moral ambiguity. The city censor boards objected to the fact that the true villain goes unpunished — a groundbreaking narrative choice for its time.

quizzes.question: What is 'Krazy Kat' originally based on?

answers.text: A newspaper comic strip by George Herriman

quizzes.explanation: Krazy Kat was originally a newspaper comic strip by George Herriman, running from 1913 to 1944. It's considered one of the greatest comic strips of all time. The strip's surreal humor and artistic style were decades ahead of their time.

quizzes.question: Who created Popeye and when did he first appear?

answers.text: E.C. Segar in 1929 (Thimble Theatre comic strip)

quizzes.explanation: Popeye was created by cartoonist E.C. Segar and first appeared on January 17, 1929 in the 'Thimble Theatre' comic strip. He was originally intended as a minor character but became so popular that he took over the entire strip.

quizzes.question: What real-world effect did the Popeye cartoons have on spinach consumption?
quizzes.explanation: Popeye's famous spinach-eating habit helped increase US spinach consumption by approximately 33%. The spinach-growing town of Crystal City, Texas even erected a statue of Popeye in 1937 to thank the character for boosting their industry.

quizzes.question: Which studio originally animated the Popeye cartoons?
quizzes.explanation: Fleischer Studios, run by brothers Max and Dave Fleischer, produced the Popeye cartoons from 1933 to 1942. The same studio also created Betty Boop and Superman cartoons. Over 231 Popeye theatrical shorts were produced by various studios.

quizzes.question: What makes the 1910 Frankenstein historically significant?

answers.text: It's the first film adaptation of Mary Shelley's novel
It's the first horror film with sound
It's the first film over 1 hour long
It's the first film to use makeup effects

quizzes.explanation: The 1910 Frankenstein, produced by Thomas Edison's studio, is the very first film adaptation of Mary Shelley's 1818 novel. It runs about 16 minutes and was directed by J. Searle Dawley. For decades, it was considered a lost film.

quizzes.question: What happened to this film for most of the 20th century?

answers.text: It was considered a lost film until a print was found
It was shown continuously in theaters
It was stored in the Library of Congress

quizzes.explanation: The 1910 Frankenstein was thought to be completely lost for decades until a print was discovered in the collection of a Wisconsin film collector in the 1970s. This rediscovery made it one of cinema's most remarkable 'found' films.

quizzes.question: Who produced the animated 'Aesop's Film Fables' series?
quizzes.explanation: Paul Terry produced the 'Aesop's Film Fables' animated series starting in 1921. Terry later founded Terrytoons studio, which created Mighty Mouse and Heckle and Jeckle. The Fables series was one of the most popular cartoon series of the 1920s.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (15/175): Felix at the Circus (1921)

quizzes.question: In what year did Felix the Cat get his signature look with the round head and wide grin?
quizzes.explanation: Animator Bill Nolan redesigned Felix in 1924, giving him the rounder, sleeker look that became his iconic appearance. The original 1919 Felix was more angular and rough. Nolan's redesign made Felix more appealing and easier to animate.

quizzes.question: What event in 1934 dramatically changed Betty Boop's character?

answers.text: The Motion Picture Production Code (Hays Code) was enforced
A new voice actress was hired

quizzes.explanation: The strict enforcement of the Hays Code in 1934 forced Fleischer Studios to dramatically tone down Betty Boop. Her revealing outfits were replaced with modest dresses, her flirtatious personality was tamed, and many fans feel she lost her distinctive charm.

videos.title: Alfred J. Kwak (1): Hurra, er ist da!

quizzes.question: Who created the character Alfred Jodocus Kwak?

answers.text: Herman van Veen (Dutch musician and entertainer)

quizzes.explanation: Alfred J. Kwak was created by Dutch musician, singer, and entertainer Herman van Veen. The animated series (1989-1991) was a Dutch-Japanese co-production and tackled serious themes like racism, dictatorship, and environmental issues through its animal characters.

quizzes.question: What real historical figure does the villain Dolf in Alfred J. Kwak represent?
quizzes.explanation: The character Dolf (a crow) is a clear allegory for Adolf Hitler — sharing a similar name, rise to power through populism, forming a political party with a black cross symbol, and attempting to take over the country. The series was remarkably bold in teaching children about fascism.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (20/175): Felix in Love (1922)

quizzes.question: What was Felix the Cat's first cartoon called?
quizzes.explanation: Felix the Cat made his debut in 'Feline Follies,' released on November 9, 1919 by Paramount Pictures. The cartoon was produced by Pat Sullivan's studio, with Otto Messmer as the primary animator and creative force behind Felix.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (30/175): Felix Saves the Day (1922)

quizzes.question: What made Felix the Cat the first major animated character in history?

answers.text: He was the first animated character to become a major merchandise star
He was the first character to appear in color
He was the first character to speak on screen
He was the first character created by Walt Disney

quizzes.explanation: Felix the Cat was the first animated character to achieve massive merchandise success — appearing on everything from toys to clocks to ceramics in the 1920s. He was essentially the 'Mickey Mouse before Mickey Mouse' and achieved worldwide fame.

quizzes.question: Who was the primary voice actress for Betty Boop?
quizzes.explanation: Mae Questel was the primary voice actress for Betty Boop, voicing her from 1931 to 1939. She won the role in a Helen Kane impersonation contest. Questel also voiced Olive Oyl in the Popeye cartoons — two of animation's most iconic female characters!

videos.title: Felix the Cat (25/175): Felix at the Fair (1922)

quizzes.question: Why did Felix the Cat's popularity decline in the late 1920s?

answers.text: Pat Sullivan refused to add sound to Felix cartoons
A rival studio bought the rights
Felix was banned in several countries

quizzes.explanation: When sound films arrived in 1927, Pat Sullivan stubbornly refused to add synchronized sound to Felix cartoons. Meanwhile, Walt Disney's Mickey Mouse debuted with sound in 'Steamboat Willie' (1928), quickly overtaking Felix as the world's most popular cartoon character.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (35/175): Felix in the Swim (1922)

quizzes.question: What famous creative tool is Felix the Cat known for using?
quizzes.explanation: Felix's 'Magic Bag of Tricks' (also called the 'Bag of Tricks') is his signature prop, introduced in the 1959 TV series. It can transform into virtually anything Felix needs — a vehicle, a tool, a weapon — making it one of animation's most versatile props.

videos.title: Little Nemo in Slumberland (1911) | Winsor McCay

quizzes.question: How many individual drawings did Winsor McCay create for 'Gertie the Dinosaur' (1914)?
quizzes.explanation: Winsor McCay drew approximately 10,000 individual drawings on rice paper for 'Gertie the Dinosaur' (1914). Gertie was the first animated character to display a personality on screen and is considered a landmark in the history of animation.

quizzes.question: What era of filmmaking does 'The Cigar Box' (1905) represent?

answers.text: The very earliest era of trick films and special effects
The era of silent feature films

quizzes.explanation: Made in 1905, 'The Cigar Box' belongs to the earliest era of cinema when filmmakers were experimenting with camera tricks and special effects. These pioneering short films laid the groundwork for all modern visual effects.

quizzes.question: When was 'Feline Follies' — Felix's debut cartoon — released?
quizzes.explanation: Feline Follies was released on November 9, 1919. The character was originally called 'Master Tom' in this first cartoon. Felix became one of the most famous cartoon characters of the 1920s — the first true animated superstar.

quizzes.question: What is the Prelinger Archives collection?

answers.text: The largest collection of ephemeral (everyday) films in the world

quizzes.explanation: The Prelinger Archives, founded by Rick Prelinger, contains over 60,000 ephemeral (advertising, educational, industrial, amateur) films. In 2002, over 2,000 films were donated to the Internet Archive and made freely available.

videos.title: Looney Tunes: A Day at the Zoo (1939)

quizzes.question: What is the origin of the name 'Looney Tunes'?
quizzes.explanation: The name 'Looney Tunes' was a deliberate play on Disney's 'Silly Symphonies.' Similarly, 'Merrie Melodies' parodied Disney's name. Warner Bros. originally created these series to promote their music catalog — hence the musical references.

videos.title: The Big Game Hunter | Classic Animation

quizzes.question: What technique was primarily used in early animation before computers?

answers.text: Hand-drawn cel animation (each frame drawn individually)

quizzes.explanation: Early cartoons like this were created using cel animation — each frame was hand-drawn on transparent celluloid sheets (cels), then photographed one by one. A 7-minute cartoon required thousands of individual drawings.

videos.title: EPIC Coca-Cola Christmas Trucks – Holidays Are Coming (1995)

quizzes.question: When did the famous Coca-Cola 'Holidays Are Coming' Christmas truck commercial first air?
quizzes.explanation: The iconic Coca-Cola Christmas truck commercial 'Holidays Are Coming' first aired in 1995 and was created by advertising agency W.B. Doner. The illuminated red trucks have become one of the most recognizable symbols of the Christmas season.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (32/175): Felix Turns the Tide (1922)

quizzes.question: Before Mickey Mouse, who was the most famous cartoon character in the world?
quizzes.explanation: Felix the Cat was the undisputed king of animated characters throughout the 1920s — the world's first animated superstar. His reign ended when he failed to transition to sound cartoons, while Mickey Mouse's 'Steamboat Willie' (1928) embraced the new technology.

quizzes.question: What dangerous stunt is Buster Keaton most famous for?

answers.text: A house facade falling around him (he stood in the open window space)
Walking on a tightrope between buildings
Riding a horse off a cliff

quizzes.explanation: In 'Steamboat Bill, Jr.' (1928), an entire house facade falls on Keaton — but he survives by standing exactly where the open window was. This stunt, done for real with no safety net, is considered one of the most dangerous stunts in film history.

videos.title: The Bee and the Rose (1908) | Segundo de Chomón

quizzes.question: Who was Segundo de Chomón?

answers.text: A Spanish cinema pioneer and visual effects innovator
A French painter who became a filmmaker

quizzes.explanation: Segundo de Chomón (1871-1929) was a Spanish filmmaker and one of cinema's earliest visual effects pioneers. He is often called 'the Spanish Méliès' for his innovative trick photography and was one of the first to use traveling matte techniques.

quizzes.question: What animation technique made the Fleischer Superman cartoons so visually stunning?
quizzes.explanation: The Fleischer brothers used extensive rotoscoping — tracing animation over live-action footage — to achieve fluid, realistic movement in the Superman cartoons. This technique, invented by Max Fleischer himself in 1915, gave Superman his lifelike movements.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (55/175): Finds a Way (1924)

quizzes.question: In the 1920s, Felix the Cat was so popular that...

answers.text: His image was painted on Charles Lindbergh's plane 'Spirit of St. Louis'
He appeared on merchandise before any other cartoon character
He starred in a Hollywood feature film
He was elected honorary mayor of a city

quizzes.explanation: Felix the Cat was the first cartoon character to become a massive merchandising phenomenon. In the 1920s, Felix appeared on everything from toys to clocks to cigarette packaging — paving the way for the merchandising empires of Mickey Mouse and beyond.

videos.title: Felix the Cat (22/175): Felix Makes Good (1922)

quizzes.question: What happened when Pat Sullivan's studio tried to compete with 'talkies'?

answers.text: Sullivan's sound Felix cartoons failed and the studio collapsed
They switched to making radio shows

quizzes.explanation: After years of refusing sound, Sullivan finally attempted sound Felix cartoons in 1929-1930, but they were poor quality and failed commercially. Sullivan fell into alcoholism and died in 1933. Felix wouldn't return to prominence until the 1959 TV series.

quizzes.question: What were 'Wochenschau' newsreels?

answers.text: German weekly cinema newsreels shown before feature films

quizzes.explanation: The 'Deutsche Wochenschau' was a weekly newsreel series shown in German cinemas before the main feature. From 1940 onwards, it became a centralized propaganda tool under Joseph Goebbels' control. Today these serve as important historical documents.

quizzes.question: Who originally created Rudolph the Red-Nosed Reindeer?

answers.text: Robert L. May for Montgomery Ward stores (1939)

quizzes.explanation: Rudolph was created in 1939 by Robert L. May, a copywriter at the Montgomery Ward department store chain, as a free Christmas coloring book for children. The famous song was written by May's brother-in-law Johnny Marks in 1949.

quizzes.question: What was the typical runtime of a theatrical cartoon short in the 1930s?
quizzes.explanation: Theatrical cartoon shorts of the 1930s-1950s typically ran about 7-8 minutes. They were designed to be shown before the main feature film in cinemas, alongside a newsreel and sometimes a live-action short — giving audiences a full entertainment program.

quizzes.question: What were 'Wochenschau' newsreels?

answers.text: Weekly cinema newsreels shown before feature films

quizzes.explanation: The 'Deutsche Wochenschau' was a weekly newsreel shown in German cinemas before the main feature. From 1940, it was centralized as a propaganda tool under Joseph Goebbels. Today they serve as crucial historical documents.

quizzes.question: Who controlled the Deutsche Wochenschau from 1940 onwards?
quizzes.explanation: From 1940, all German newsreel companies were merged into the centralized 'Deutsche Wochenschau GmbH' under direct control of Goebbels' Propaganda Ministry. Content was carefully curated to shape public opinion.

quizzes.question: How often were Wochenschau newsreels produced?
quizzes.explanation: 'Wochenschau' literally translates to 'weekly show.' Each edition covered the past week's events, running 20-40 minutes. Cinema attendance was mandatory in some periods, making newsreels a powerful tool for mass communication.

quizzes.question: What were 'Wochenschau' newsreels?

answers.text: Weekly cinema newsreels shown before feature films

quizzes.explanation: The 'Deutsche Wochenschau' was a weekly newsreel shown in German cinemas before the main feature. From 1940, it was centralized as a propaganda tool under Joseph Goebbels. Today they serve as crucial historical documents.

quizzes.question: Who controlled the Deutsche Wochenschau from 1940 onwards?
quizzes.explanation: From 1940, all German newsreel companies were merged into the centralized 'Deutsche Wochenschau GmbH' under direct control of Goebbels' Propaganda Ministry. Content was carefully curated to shape public opinion.

quizzes.question: Where does 'Der kleine Maulwurf' (Krtek) originally come from?
quizzes.explanation: Der kleine Maulwurf (Krtek) was created by Czech animator Zdenek Miler in 1956. The first episode 'How the Mole Got His Trousers' won a Silver Lion at the Venice Film Festival!

quizzes.question: Who created the little mole character?
quizzes.explanation: Zdenek Miler (1921-2011) created the little mole in 1956. He was inspired when he tripped over a molehill during a walk! Miler directed all episodes over 46 years until his retirement.

quizzes.question: Why does the little mole almost never speak?

answers.text: So children worldwide can understand without translation
The studio couldn't afford voice actors
It was required by Czech censorship

quizzes.explanation: Zdenek Miler deliberately made the mole nearly speechless (only saying 'Hello!' in Czech) so children of ALL countries could enjoy the cartoons without translation. This made Krtek popular from Japan to Germany!

quizzes.question: What real historical figure does villain Dolf represent?
quizzes.explanation: Dolf (a crow) is a clear allegory for Adolf Hitler - sharing a similar name, rise to power through populism, a political party with a black cross symbol, and attempted takeover of the country. A bold choice for a children's show!

quizzes.question: What was Betty Boop originally designed as?
quizzes.explanation: Betty Boop first appeared on August 9, 1930 in 'Dizzy Dishes' as a French poodle with floppy dog ears. She was gradually redesigned into her iconic human form, with her dog ears becoming hoop earrings.

quizzes.question: Which event in 1934 dramatically changed Betty Boop's character?

answers.text: The Hays Code (Motion Picture Production Code)
The studio was sold to Disney

quizzes.explanation: The strict enforcement of the Hays Code in 1934 forced Fleischer Studios to dramatically tone down Betty Boop. Her revealing outfits were replaced with modest dresses and her flirtatious personality was tamed.

quizzes.question: Who was Betty Boop's primary voice actress?
quizzes.explanation: Mae Questel voiced Betty Boop from 1931-1939. She won the role in a Helen Kane impersonation contest! Questel also voiced Olive Oyl in the Popeye cartoons - two of animation's most iconic female characters.

quizzes.explanation: Betty Boop first appeared on August 9, 1930 in 'Dizzy Dishes' as a French poodle with floppy dog ears. She was gradually redesigned into her iconic human form, with her dog ears becoming hoop earrings.

quizzes.question: Which event in 1934 dramatically changed Betty Boop's character?

answers.text: The Hays Code (Motion Picture Production Code)
The studio was sold to Disney

quizzes.explanation: The strict enforcement of the Hays Code in 1934 forced Fleischer Studios to dramatically tone down Betty Boop. Her revealing outfits were replaced with modest dresses and her flirtatious personality was tamed.

quizzes.question: Who was Betty Boop's primary voice actress?
quizzes.explanation: Mae Questel voiced Betty Boop from 1931-1939. She won the role in a Helen Kane impersonation contest! Questel also voiced Olive Oyl in the Popeye cartoons - two of animation's most iconic female characters.

quizzes.question: What are 'Soundies'?

answers.text: Music videos from the 1940s played on jukebox-like machines

quizzes.explanation: Soundies were short musical films from the 1940s, played on coin-operated 'Panoram' machines in bars, restaurants, and nightclubs. They are considered the direct ancestors of modern music videos!

quizzes.question: On what kind of machine were Soundies originally shown?
quizzes.explanation: The Mills Panoram was a coin-operated movie jukebox with a built-in rear-projection screen. For 10 cents, you could watch a 3-minute musical film. The Panoram was manufactured 1940-1946 with about 12,000 machines produced.
